coredata.dc:title: Facile incorporation of dispersed fluorescent carbon nanodots into mesoporous silica nanosphere for pH-triggered drug delivery and imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yi
Shi, Wei
Wang, Shanshan
Li, Chengyi
Qian, Min
Chen, Jian
Huang, Rongqin
coredata.dc:description: Dispersed incorporation of functional nanoparticles into ordered mesopores to exert their synergistic properties has shown a promising application, but proved to be a challenging task. Herein, a facile strategy based on solid-phase transformation of the template into polymer-coated carbonaceous nanodots (pCNDs) is used for direct synthesis of multifunctional mesoporous silica nanospheres (MFMSNs) incorporated with dispersed fluorescent carbon nanodots. The homogeneous pCNDs incorporated into mesoporous silica nanospheres (MSNs) can simultaneously act as the nontoxic fluorescence probe for highly efficient fluorescence imaging and also anchoring sites for anticancer drug loading. On the basis of the special hydrophobic π–π/electrostatic interaction between the confined pCNDs in MFMSNs and the aromatic drug molecules, MFMSNs could also function as an advanced drug carrier for pH-triggered drug release. The very simple strategy suggests new insights for synthesizing multifunctional mesoporous nanomaterials and extends the biomedical functions of traditional MSN-based nanovehicles for future biomedical applications.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the grants from the National Key Basic Research Program of China ( 2013CB932502 , 973 Program), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21303022 and 81573002 ), Sino-German Research Project ( GZ995 ), and Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai City of China ( 13ZR1451400 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image, (B) TEM image, (C) HRTEM image, (D) EDX pattern, (E) STEM image, and (F) EDX mapping of MFMSNs. Inset in (A) shows particle size distribution curve of MFMSNs. The arrows in (C) indicate the incorporated carbon nanodots. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Small-angle XRD pattern, (B) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm and the corresponding pore size distribution curve (inset), and (C) XPS survey spectrum and the corresponding C1s spectrum (inset) of MFMSNs. (D) UV−Vis absorption (Abs) and excitation-dependent emission (Em) spectra of the MFMSNs in aqueous solutions. (E, F) Photographs of the MFMSNs aqueous solution excited by daylight and a 365-nm UV lamp. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Gradual changes in blue–green and red fluorescence when increasing DOX/MFMSNs ratios from 0:1 to 1:1. (B) Cumulative release profiles of DOX from MFMSNs at different pHs. (C–F) pH-stimulated release of DOX in U251 cells. Cells were incubated with MFMSNDs after pretreatment without (C, D) and with (E, F) bafilomycin A1. The enhancement of the intralysosomal pH caused by bafilomycin A1 resulted in the decreased release of DOX. C and E: the bright field images of D and E, respectively. Red: DOX. Bar = 75 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Confocal microscopy images of U251 cells incubated with MFMSNs for different time periods. BF: bright field images. Green: MFMSNs, Merge: BF-MFMSNs. Bar = 10 μm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A–T) Confocal microscopy images of U251 cells incubated with MFMSNDs for different time periods. BF: bright field images. Green: MFMSNs, Red: DOX, Merge-1: MFMSNDs, Merge-2: BF-MFMSNDs. Bar = 100 μm. (U) Cytotoxicity curves of MFMSNs and MFMSNDs obtained via the CCK-8 test. U251 glioma cells were treated with MFMSNs or MFMSNDs at different concentrations for 24 h. Data were expressed as mean ± S.D. (n = 4). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Facile incorporation of dispersed fluorescent carbon nanodots into mesoporous silica nanosphere for pH-triggered drug delivery and imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Neurosurgery, Affiliated Hospital of Nantong University, Nantong, 226001, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Key Laboratory of Smart Drug Delivery, Ministry of Education, Fudan University, Shanghai, 201203, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Smart Drug Delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Analysis and Measurement, Fudan University, Shanghai, 200433, People’s Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dispersed incorporation of functional nanoparticles into ordered mesopores to exert their synergistic properties has shown a promising application, but proved to be a challenging task. Herein, a facile strategy based on solid-phase transformation of the template into polymer-coated carbonaceous nanodots (pCNDs) is used for direct synthesis of multifunctional mesoporous silica nanospheres (MFMSNs) incorporated with dispersed fluorescent carbon nanodots. The homogeneous pCNDs incorporated into mesoporous silica nanospheres (MSNs) can simultaneously act as the nontoxic fluorescence probe for highly efficient fluorescence imaging and also anchoring sites for anticancer drug loading. On the basis of the special hydrophobic π–π/electrostatic interaction between the confined pCNDs in MFMSNs and the aromatic drug molecules, MFMSNs could also function as an advanced drug carrier for pH-triggered drug release. The very simple strategy suggests new insights for synthesizing multifunctional mesoporous nanomaterials and extends the biomedical functions of traditional MSN-based nanovehicles for future biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Combining diagnostic probes (imaging agents) and chemotherapy drugs in one carrier for controllable cancer therapy has attracted extensive research attention because of their advantages such as avoiding insufficient or excessive drug dosing, real-time monitoring therapeutic progression, and minimizing invasive damages to normal tissues [1–3]. Various drug vehicles (e.g., polymers, carbon nanomaterials and mesoporous nanoparticles) functionalized by imaging agents, such as magnetic nanoparticles, radionuclides, fluorescent organic dyes, upconversion nanoparticles, or semiconductor nanocrystals, have been developed for imaging-guided cancer chemotherapy [4–9]. Among all of these platforms, mesoporous silica nanospheres (MSNs) is a widely studied vector because of their high surface area, large pore volume with tunable pore sizes, facility for surface modification, and hydrophilicity [10–12]. Furthermore, optical fluorescent probes have advantages such as high sensitivity, high spatial resolution, and ease of use [13,14]. Thus, binding MSN-based nanomaterials with fluorescent probes, as drug carriers, is the most prevalent theranostic strategy [15,16]. However, in most of these strategies, the fluorescent probes are usually centralized in MSNs or conjugated on their surfaces [17–19], which are not beneficial to load drugs and exploit the synergetic mesoporous optical properties, and also these processes need labor-intensive preparations and complex modifications [20,21]. Furthermore, although some organical fluorescent dyes with low molecular weight can be introduced into mesoporous silica by a cocondensation method, or some large molecules (e.g, polymers [22], carbon nanodots [23], and rare-earth nanocrystals [24]) can be grafted onto mesoporous silica by post-synthesis, the direct incorporation of multifunctional inorganic precursors (such as carbon nanodots) with relatively high molecular weight into MSNs is still a challenging task [25]. In particular, incorporation of dispersed carbon nanodots into MSNs with full exposure of their “active sites” to the guest molecules (such as drugs) will be an ideal drug delivery platform, because it can effectively exploit advantages of both carbon nanodots (imaging and pH-triggered drug release) and MSNs (high drug loading capacity and biocompatibility).

Therefore, in this study, we developed a simple method to solid-phase transform the surfactant, a toxic template with difficulty for removal, into fluorescent polymer-coated carbonaceous nanodots (pCNDs), which were consequently dispersedly incorporated into the MSNs to form multifunctional fluorescent mesoporous silica nanospheres (MFMSNs) for the imaging-guided drug delivery with pH-responsiveness.

Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was purchased from Shanghai Mingzhi Chemical Co., Ltd (China). Doxorubicin (DOX) was purchased from Huafeng United Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was obtained from Dojindo Laboratories (Japan). Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Gibco (Life Technologies, CA, USA). Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), lithium chloride (LiCl), and other reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, U.S.A.). All the chemicals were used without further purification.

CTAB-containing MSNs were synthesized according to our previous report. Typically, surfactant solution prepared by dissolving 100 mg of CTAB in aqueous NaOH (14.5 mM, 48 ml) was heated to 80 °C, and then slowly added with TEOS (500 ml). After stirring for 2 h, the recovered solid product was mildly washed to remove the loosely adsorbed surfactant on the particle surfaces, filtered, and freeze-dried. For the synthesis of MFMSNs, 100 mg of CTAB-containing MSNs were dissolved in the 250-μl solution of 20 wt% NaCl, 5 wt% LiCl, 5 wt% KNO3, and 80 wt% H2O for over 12 h to obtain a dry gel. After freeze-drying, the dry gel became a white powder, which was placed in the pipe furnace and calcined at 250–300 °C for 2–3 h under air atmosphere. Then, the obtained yellow powder was dispersed in water by ultrasonication and dialyzed against water (MW cutoff = 20000) for 72 h to remove the residual salts and loosely adsorbed pCNDs. Finally, the solid in the dialysis bag was recovered as MFMSNs by centrifugation (to remove large nanoparticles) and freeze-drying. For the synthesis of pure pCNDs, MFMSNs were stirred in the NaOH solution (4 mol/L) for about 4 h. Then, the pH of the solution was adjusted to alkalescence by HCl solution and dilution. After that, the mixture was dialyzed against water (MW cutoff = 3500) to remove the residual impurities. Finally, the solid in the dialysis bag was recovered as pCNDs.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) were performed using Philips XL-30 and Hitachi S-4800 scanning electron microscope, respectively. The particle size distribution was counted statistically according to the SEM images. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (DF-STEM), and scanning energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS-Mapping) were performed on a JEM-2100F instrument with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. For TEM observation, the samples were dispersed in ethanol and supported on a lacey support film on a Cu grid. Small angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) measurements were taken on a Nanostar U SAXS system (Bruker, Germany) using Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 35 mA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Rigaku D/MAX-RB diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation at 40 kV and 60 mA. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a RBD upgraded PHI-5000C ESCA system (Perkin Elmer) with Al Ka radiation (hυ = 1486.6 eV) as the X-ray source for excitation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed on a Nicolet-670 FTIR spectrometer using the KBr pellet method. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K and pore size distribution curves were measured using Tristar 3000 systems. The BET method was used to calculate the surface area. Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis of the sample was performed on a Pyris Diamond TG/DTA thermogravimetric analyzer. The sample was heated from room temperature to 850 °C at the speed of 10 °C/min under air atmosphere. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained on a UV–vis absorption spectrometer (UV759, China). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). The mean diameter and zeta potential of MFMSNs were determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Malvern NanoZS zeta potential/particle sizer. Optical images of different sample solutions were taken using a common camera.

In order to load drug molecules (DOX) into the mesopores of MFMSNs, 500 μl of MFMSNs ethanol solution (400 μg/ml) was mixed with 500 μl of DOX ethanol solution (500 μg/ml) and stirred at room temperature for over 12 h. The mixture was centrifuged and the supernatant was removed. The DOX-loaded nanoparticles were dried under vacuum to remove trace ethanol and rinsed in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH7.4) to remove the loosely adsorbed DOX. Finally, the recovered solids (MFMSNDs) were resuspended in different solutions for further experiments. In order to determine the loading capacity, all the removed supernatants were collected and the absorption at 480 nm was measured. The drug loading capacity was calculated as follows: Drug loading capacity (%) = (weight of added drug − weight of removed drug)/weight of MFMSNs × 100%.

The release of DOX from MFMSNDs was studied at 37 °C in three different PBSs (pH 5.0, 6.0, and 7.4). Briefly, MFMSNDs (contained 0.2 mg MFMSNs) was dispersed in 1 ml of PBS, and then transferred into a dialysis bag (MW cutoff = 3500). The dialysis bag was immersed in 30 ml of PBS with different pH values at 37 °C under constant shaking (150 rpm). At determined time points, aliquots of 10-ml dialysis solution were taken out for fluorescence measurement and replenished with the same volume of the corresponding fresh buffer. The standard curves of DOX in different PBSs were established and the amount of released DOX was measured by fluorescence spectroscopy. The cumulative release of DOX was determined as a percentage compared to the loaded DOX within MFMSNDs.

Human glioma cell line U251 cells were obtained from Shanghai Cell Bank, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences. The U251 cells were maintained routinely in DMEM media supplemented with 10% FBS, 1% l-glutamine, 1% penicillin, and 1% streptomycin stock solutions at 37 °C in 5% CO2. The media were changed every 2 days, and the cells were passaged by trypsinization before confluence.

The U251 cells were cultured in 24-well plates with 200 μl of media at a density of 4 × 104 cells/well for 2 days. For time-dependent cellular uptake experiment, MFMSNDs with the MFMSNs concentration at 100 μg/ml was added into the cell wells and incubated for 6, 12, 18 and 24 h. In order to evaluate the concentration-dependent cellular uptake, MFMSNDs with MFMSNs concentrations of 50, 100, and 150 μg/ml, was incubated with cells for 18 h. Then, the cells were rinsed twice carefully. Confocal images were obtained using a Zeiss LSM710 microscope. Repetitive studies were carried out and three independent fields were observed in each study.

In order to evaluate intracellular pH-responsive drug release, bafilomycin A1 was used as an agent to prevent acidification and thus increase pH within acid compartments. Briefly, the U251 cells were seeded in 96-well microplates at a density of 8 × 103 cells/well for 2 days. After rinsing with PBS, MFMSNDs with MFMSNs concentration of 150 μg/ml was added to the wells and incubated for 4 h. In some wells, the cells were pretreated with bafilomycin A1 (100 nM) for 1 h. Then, they were rinsed again with PBS, fixed with 4% formaldehyde for 15 min, and examined under a confocal microscope. Repetitive studies were carried out and three independent fields were observed in each study.

Cytotoxicity of U251 glioma cells was investigated using CCK-8 assay. The U251 cells were cultured in 96-well microplates with 100 μl of media at a density of 2 × 104 cells/well for 1 day. MFMSNs or MFMSNDs with different MFMSNs concentrations were added separately into wells and incubated for 24 h, following which the culture media were replaced with fresh ones. Then, 10 μl of CCK-8 solution was added to each well and incubated for another 2 h at 37 °C. After that, the absorbance was measured with a microplate reader at 450 nm. Viability of treated cell wells was expressed as a percentage of the viability of unexposed wells.

The surfactant CTAB is a widely used template and resides within the mesopore channels during the synthesis of MSNs. After catalyzed by LiCl, KCl, NaCl and KNO3, CTAB-contained MSNs were solid-phase transformed into MFMSNs first by possible oxidation–condensation reaction of CTAB in MSNs under high temperature and air atmosphere. The morphology and microstructure of MFMSNs and common MSNs were observed by SEM, TEM, HRTEM, SAXS pattern, and small-angle X-ray diffraction (sXRD) pattern (Fig. 1 and Fig. S1). It was found that MFMSNs exhibited and maintained uniform spherical morphology with a diameter of about 115 nm and several mesopores with a size of 2.4 nm (Fig. 1A–C and Fig. S1A–C), which were similar to those of common MSNs (Fig. S1D–F). Moreover, an enhanced and high angle-shifted (100) diffraction of MFMSNs in SAXS pattern as compared to that of MSNs suggested the increased framework density with shrunk unit cell under the high temperature treatment [26]. However, the two-dimensional hexagonal P6mm structure as revealed by the three typical diffraction peaks ((100), (110), and (200)) with reciprocal spacing ratios (1/d
hk ) of 1:√3:2 (Fig. 2 A) [27] suggested the reservation of the ordered mesopores. By contrast, additional dot-like nanoparticles were emerged and embedded in MFMSNs as observed in Fig. 1B, C and Fig. S1B, C, implying the incorporation of small nanoparticles into MSNs. Further characterizations by STEM image, EDX pattern and mapping proved the presence of carbonaceous nanoparticles within MFMSNs, and the carbon species were dispersed all over MFMSNs (Fig. 1D–F and Fig. S2), which can be attributed to the homogeneous incorporation of carbonaceous nanoparticles within the mesopore silica network due to the solid-phase transformation of CTAB template located in every mesopore. Results of TG and thermogravimetry–mass spectrometry (TG-MS) in Fig. S3 showed that the carbonaceous nanoparticles within MFMSNs constituted 26 wt% and were mainly composed of C and N elements, which might be N-contained carbon nanodots. XPS of MFMSNs also demonstrated the presence of C, O, N species (carbonaceous nanoparticles) in MSNs (Fig. 2C), and corresponding C1s spectrum (inset in Fig. 2C) can be deconvoluted into three typical peaks attributed to CC/CC (284.5 eV), CO/CN (286.1 eV), and OCO (288.8 eV) [28,29]. This suggested that the carbonaceous nanoparticles within MSNs had plentiful surface functional groups such as carboxyls (COOH) and amidos (NH2), which could also be verified by the FTIR spectra in Fig. S4. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and corresponding pore diameter distribution curve in Fig. 2B revealed that MFMSNs still had a high Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 855 m2 g−1, a pore volume of 0.71 cm3 g−1, and a most probable pore size of 2.4 nm. These values, especially the pore volume, were smaller than that of MSNs, which also suggested the incorporation of the carbon nanodots into the mesopores (Fig. S5 and Table S1).

In order to further elucidate the formation of N-contained carbon nanodots within MSNs, Si-extracted MFMSNs (only the pure pCNDs) were carefully characterized. TEM images showed that Si-extracted MFMSNs were small nanoparticles with size of about 2–10 nm (Fig. S6), which was in agreement with the DLS result (Fig. S7). These nanoparticles exhibited a polymer coating as observed from the HRTEM images (Fig. S6C). The relative larger sizes of some pure pCNDs as compared to the mesopore diameter might be attributed to (1) their easy aggregation caused by the polymer coating without MSNs support and (2) the carbon nanodots formed by the solid-phase transformation of surface-adsorbed CTAB (Fig. S6). XPS and IR results also showed that the polymer-coated carbon nanodots were composed of C-, N-, and O-containing functional groups with the atomic percentage of 80 ± 3.5% C, 18 ± 2.6% O, and 2 ± 0.8% N, respectively (Figs. S4 and S8). These results confirmed the solid-phase transformation of CTAB into the pCNDs, which was consequently dispersedly incorporated within MSNs. This might be an “solid-phase oxidation–condensation” process assisted by catalysts and air atmosphere, and it reserved most of the primary mesopores but made the pCNDs uniformly distribute within the whole MSNs.

Because of the homogeneous incorporation of pCNDs within the mesopore network, MFMSNs exhibited a broad UV–Vis absorption at 200–700 nm and strong blue–green luminescence under excitation of a 365-nm UV lamp (Fig. 2D–F). The emission spectra of MFMSNs ranged from blue (460 nm) to green (540 nm), with a dependence on excitation wavelengths, which was similar to that of pure pCNDs (Si-extracted sample) (Fig. S9) and some other specially prepared carbon dots [30,31]. This fluorescence behavior may be attributed to the synergistic effect of the quantum dimensions and surface/molecular state of the confined pCNDs, which affected the band gap and contributed to the complexity of the excited states [32]. Furthermore, MFMSNs had high dispersity as evidenced by DLS result (Fig. S10), which resulted in a mean diameter of 169 nm, only slightly higher than that measured by TEM due to the hydrophilization [33,34]. Furthermore, the colloidal stability results showed that the pure pCNDs possess excellent dispersity (not any sedimentation after standing for 24 h) in PBS 5.0, 6.0, and 7.4 because of the presence of hydrophilic groups on their surfaces (Fig. S11). Incorporating the pCNDs into MSNs also endowed MFMSNs with high dispersity in PBS solutions with different pH values as revealed by no obvious sedimentation after standing for 24 h (Fig. S11). The stable dispersity of MFMSNs could be attributed to both the hydrophilic effects of the silanols from MSNs and several carboxyls/amidos from pCNDs. Furthermore, the incorporated pCNDs, especially those confined in the silica matrix, was stable during long time incubation unless excluded from MSN by the Si-dissolution, as shown by the compared fluorescence intensities under successively different treatments (Fig. S12). Therefore, MFMSNs were expected to be good drug vehicles. In addition, the zeta potential of MFMSNs solution was −31.2 mV, which, assisted with the incorporated carbonaceous pCNDs, was beneficial for loading the electropositive anticancer drugs with size smaller than the pore diameter, such as DOX via electrostatic interaction and π–π stacking. Therefore, the DOX molecules could be adsorbed by the confined pCNDs through the mesopores of MSNs, and a loading capacity of 0.63 ± 0.04 mg/mg was achieved in the given experimental conditions, which was a little higher than that of MSNs (0.53 ± 0.05 mg/mg) in spite of its decreased pore volume. This might be attributed to the increased adsorption sites produced by the confined pCNDs. Moreover, the fluorescence spectra of different DOX-incubated MFMSNs samples clearly exhibited a decrease in blue–green fluorescence (440–500 nm) and an increase in red fluorescence (530–650 nm) with the increase of DOX/MFMSNs ratios (Fig. 3 A). This is also easily observed by the naked eye under UV excitation (Fig. S13). These results suggested the sensitive fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) but not the interference between DOX and the incorporated pCNDs, which confirmed the direct contact and distinctive electrostatic/π–π interactions [35,36]. Therefore, the unique properties of MFMSNs, including the high emission wavelength and the special drug carrier interactions, will be much more useful in multicolor imaging and drug delivery applications.

The capability of controllable drug delivery of MFMSNs was firstly demonstrated by the pH-dependent DOX release via only free DOX molecules penetrating dialysis membrane. As shown in Fig. 3B, at pH 7.4, the cumulative release increased slowly, exhibiting a low value of about 20% over the course of 12 h. A substantial increase of drug release rate was observed in acidic solutions, in which the release rate in pH 5.0 solution was higher than that in pH 6.0 solution. At pH 5.0, the cumulative release reached about 65% over the course of 12 h. Moreover, the pH-responsive DOX release from MFMSNDs was confirmed in cellular level, where an obvious decrease of DOX fluorescent signal (decreased release) was observed in the glioma U251 cells pretreated with bafilomycin A1 (Fig. 3C–F). This is because bafilomycin A1 is a widely used inhibitor of the vacuolar type H+-ATPase. It can prevent the acidification of lysosome and cause the intralysosomal pH increasing from 5.3 to 6.3 [33]. The enhanced release in low pH could be attributed to the reduced hydrophobic π–π interaction and electrostatic interaction between DOX and MFMSNs, which consequently resulted in the dissociation of DOX from the directly contacted pCNDs in MFMSNs [35,36]. The pH-dependent drug release behavior can minimize the latent damage to normal cells, but enhance the therapeutic anti-cancer effect based on the tumor acidic microenvironment and intracellular acidic endosomes and lysosomes [37]. Notably, owing to the intrinsic premature release at pH 7.4 for uncapped mesoporous silica [38,39], the pH responsiveness observed here was modest but could be enhanced by many methods, including pH-triggered organic coatings to seal the pores after the drug loading or leaching out the loosely held DOX at pH 7.4 prior to use [40–42].

The cytotoxicities of the material MFMSNs and the drug-loaded system MFMSNDs were also evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5U, for 24-h incubation, MFMSNs exhibited no apparent cytotoxicity up to 400 μg/ml, whose viability was maintained at > 92%. For MFMSNDs, the cytotoxicity to U251 cells significantly decreased with the concentration. When the concentration reached 100 μg/ml, the viability of U251 cells decreased to 26.02 ± 2.89%, demonstrating the high cell-killing effect of the drug delivery system.

MFMSNs were prepared by a facile strategy. In this strategy, the surfactant, a toxic template with difficulty of removal during the synthesis of MSNs, was solid phase transformed into fluorescent pCNDs, and consequently incorporated into MSNs. Because of the bright blue–green fluorescence, high biocompatibility, and the full exposure of “active sites” (pCNDs) to the drug molecules, MFMSNs can simultaneously deliver aromatic drugs into cancer cells with pH-responsive release and achieve efficient fluorescence imaging with high resolution. Further modification of MFMSNs for the in vivo application and exploitation of real-time monitoring for drug delivery based on the FRET is still under study. The simple strategy for facile incorporation of dispersed fluorescent carbon nanodots into MSNs and the significantly extending biomedical applications of traditional MSNs-based nanovehicles may suggest new insights for synthesizing multi-functional mesoporous nanomaterials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the grants from the National Key Basic Research Program of China (2013CB932502, 973 Program), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21303022 and 81573002), Sino-German Research Project (GZ995), and Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai City of China (13ZR1451400).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.07.009.
